answer:
In the Urdu poetic register of the Falsafa corpus—exemplified by Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* and Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*—**'bulbul'** is the nightingale (*Pycnonotus cafer*, a bulbuls species common in South Asia), but it functions as a **conventional symbol of the passionate lover, poet, or soul in longing**. Its song (*naala* or lament) evokes unrequited love, sorrow (*gham*), and spiritual yearning, often contrasted with the *gul* (rose, symbolizing the beloved). A true bulbul is defined by enduring hardship (*khizaan*, autumn), as without sorrow, "woh bulbul hi nahin" ("it cannot truly be").[^1]

This motif recurs in garden (*chaman*) scenes: the bulbul pines for, sings to, or perishes for the gul, mirroring the lover's ecstasy and pain.[^2][^3] Iqbal addresses the bulbul directly as a seeker ("ae bulbul"), elevating it to a model of desire fulfilled only through divine or existential striving.[^4] Ghalib casts it as Zulaikha (the Qur'anic lover of Joseph/Yusuf), desperately enamored of the rose exalted "in the bazaar of the garden."[^5]

The corpus lacks a prose lexicon defining it outright (it is a *rish* or stock image from Persianate ghazal tradition), but usage confirms this symbolic role across Iqbal's philosophical verses on sorrow, union (*visal*), and awakening.[^6]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 7 (*Falsafa-e-Gham*), [p-c49e89](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/transliteration/#p-c49e89).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 35 (*Rukhsat Ae Bazm-e-Jahan*), [p-2e0f9d](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/35-rukhsat-ae-bazm-e-jahan/translation/#p-2e0f9d).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 20 (*Shikwa*), [p-bee7c4](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/transliteration/#p-bee7c4). (One bulbul remains amid a desolate garden, its breast tempestuous with song.)

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 27 (*Visal*), [p-f82112](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/transliteration/#p-f82112).

[^5]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 15 (*Barshkal-e-girya-e-aashiq hai dekha chahiye*), [p-452161](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/15-barshkal-e-girya-e-aashiq-hai-dekha-chahiye/translation/#p-452161).

[^6]: Aggregated from 20+ *bulbul* instances in *Bang-e-Dara* (Parts 1–3); see also ch. 6 (Part 1) [p-b7c46d](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/06-kushaada-dast-e-karam-jab-woh-beniyaaz-kare/translation/#p-b7c46d) on its "lament" distinguishing rose from thorn.
